coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of quinolone antibiotics in milk based on N and S co-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fang
Li, Xiaoyun
Zhang, Yuchen
Li, Hui
Jiang, Shanxue
Han, Jiajun
Gong, Wenwen
Li, Dandan
Yao, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Quinolone antibiotics are widely used to prevent and treat diseases caused by bacterial infection. However, overuse of antibiotics may lead to their residue in the environment and food, posing potential threats to human health. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) was synthesized, which can realize the detection of moxifloxacin (MXF), gatifloxacin (GAT) and ofloxacin (OFX) in milk. After addition of these antibiotics, the fluorescence at 440 nm originating from N, S-CQDs was significantly quenched due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the fluorescence at 525 nm (MXF), 500 nm (GAT) and 515 nm (OFX) were enhanced due to aggregation induced emission (AIE). The fluorescence changes were completed within 30 s and stabilized for at least 14 days. Good linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440 for MXF, F500/F440 for GAT and F515/F440 for OFX) and the antibiotic concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 100 mg L−1 were obtained, with R2 above 0.99. The limits of quantitation were in the range of 1.67–6.20 μg L−1, and MXF, GAT and OFX can be well identified by linear discriminant analysis. The probe enabled quantitative analysis of MXF, GAT and OFX in milk samples, with recoveries ranging from 82% to 107%. A test strip based on the ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully applied for the detection of MXF and GAT in milk in combination with a smartphone. This method exerted high specificity and sensitivity, which has the potential to achieve in-site detection of MXF, GAT and OFX in milk.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Moxifloxacin
Gatifloxacin
Ofloxacin
Milk

Smartphone-assisted detection based on fluorescent test strip
Ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of quinolone antibiotics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV absorbance and fluorescence spectra; (B) FTIR spectrum; (C)TEM image and particle size distribution; (D) XRD image; and (E) XPS spectrum of N, S-CQDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CQDs after addition of different concentrations of (A) MXF, (B) GAT and (C) OFX.
Discrimination result of the fluorescence response identification results of MXF, GAT, and OFX in (A) aqueous solution and (B) milk samples.

Fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440) of N, S-CQDs (A) within 30 min; (B) at different pH; (C) within 14 days at room temperature; and (D) within 14 days at 4 °C after addition of MXF, GAT, and OFX.
Fluorescence intensity ratios of (A) F525/F440; (B) F500/F440; and (C) F515/F440 after addition of 5 mg L−1 MXF, GAT, and OFX and 50 mg L−1 antibiotics, pesticides or metal ions.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N, S-CQDs before and after addition of MXF, GAT and OFX; (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CQDs, and ultraviolet absorbance spectra of MXF, GAT and OFX.
Fluorescence test strip results of (A) MXF and (B) OFX in milk samples under UV lamp.
Comparison of MXF, GAT, and OFX detection methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CuNPs represented nano-flower structured copper nanoparticles.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cysteic acid/CPE represented cysteic acid modified carbon paste electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: β-CD/rGO/GCE represented β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) and reduced-graphene oxide (rGO) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MoS2@MWCNT represented molybdenum disulfide nanostructure grown on multi-walled carbon nanotube.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: i
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CFO/GCE represented disposable glassy carbon electrode modified by ceramic cerium ferrite CeFeO3.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: j
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CuS-rGO/g-C3N4-GCE represented reduced graphene oxide-copper sulfide-carbon nitride nanosheets composite.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: m
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Gl@Cu NCs represented copper nanoclusters coated with sodium gluconate.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: n
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs represented CQDs synthesized from 2,3-diaminonaphthalene and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: o
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: T-GQDs represented tri-doped graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of quinolone antibiotics in milk based on N and S co-doped carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quinolone antibiotics are widely used to prevent and treat diseases caused by bacterial infection. However, overuse of antibiotics may lead to their residue in the environment and food, posing potential threats to human health. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) was synthesized, which can realize the detection of moxifloxacin (MXF), gatifloxacin (GAT) and ofloxacin (OFX) in milk. After addition of these antibiotics, the fluorescence at 440 nm originating from N, S-CQDs was significantly quenched due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the fluorescence at 525 nm (MXF), 500 nm (GAT) and 515 nm (OFX) were enhanced due to aggregation induced emission (AIE). The fluorescence changes were completed within 30 s and stabilized for at least 14 days. Good linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440 for MXF, F500/F440 for GAT and F515/F440 for OFX) and the antibiotic concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 100 mg L−1 were obtained, with R2 above 0.99. The limits of quantitation were in the range of 1.67–6.20 μg L−1, and MXF, GAT and OFX can be well identified by linear discriminant analysis. The probe enabled quantitative analysis of MXF, GAT and OFX in milk samples, with recoveries ranging from 82% to 107%. A test strip based on the ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully applied for the detection of MXF and GAT in milk in combination with a smartphone. This method exerted high specificity and sensitivity, which has the potential to achieve in-site detection of MXF, GAT and OFX in milk.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quinolone antibiotics are a class of drugs with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, which are widely used to prevent and treat diseases caused by bacterial infection in humans and animals, such as moxifloxacin (MXF), gatifloxacin (GAT) and ofloxacin (OFX) (A. Chen et al., 2022; J.-Y. Li et al., 2024; Song et al., 2024). However, quinolone antibiotics are stable and difficult to be degraded and metabolized in the environment and organisms. Overuse of quinolone antibiotics may lead to their residues in the environment and foods of animal origin, such as meat, eggs and milk (Chomthong et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024; R. Sharma et al., 2024). For instance, the maximum residual concentration of quinolone antibiotics in vegetable soils in Chongqing, China was 485.73 μg kg−1 (Fang et al., 2023). Quinolone antibiotics were found in the migratory goat milk in India, with the concentrations in the range of 43.3–115.1 μg L−1 (A. Sharma & Kumar, 2024). It has been reported that MXF and GAT at actual environmental concentrations could promote the release of microcystin by Microcystis aeruginosa (Wan et al., 2021). GAT could impair the cardiovascular system function of zebrafish, which induce pericardial edema and cardiac dysfunction (Wan et al., 2021). Exposure of OFX affected nine successive generations (F1-F9) of Caenorhabditis elegans, with stimulating effect on F2 to F4 and inhibiting effect on F5 to F9 generation (J. Zhang, Zheng, & Yu, 2022). It can be seen that quinolone antibiotic residues may pose enormous threats to environment and human health. Therefore, analysis of quinolone antibiotics in the environment and food is crucial for health risk assessment.

Traditional analytical methods can be used to detect quinolone antibiotics in the environment and food, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis and electrochemical methods (He, Lv, Yu, & Feng, 2010; Kheirandish et al., 2023; Yan, Zhou, Wang, & Huang, 2023; Yao, Song, Yan, & Chen, 2023). However, these methods commonly require complex and time-consuming pretreatment followed by usage of large instruments to achieve analysis, which cannot meet the demand for timely detection of perishable food such as milk (Balabin & Smirnov, 2011). Therefore, the development of a rapid, sensitive and simple method for the detection of quinolone antibiotics in milk is of great significance for food safety and human health.

Fluorescence analysis has received extensive attention because of its advantages such as simple operation, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and rapidity (C.-Y. Wang, Sun, et al., 2022; Zhai et al., 2024). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered ideal fluorescent probes for the detection of contaminants in the environment and food due to their high fluorescence stability, good biocompatibility and low cost (Cao et al., 2024; Y. Wang, Qin, et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023). CQDs can be synthesized from a variety of carbon-containing materials. Doping with heteroatoms (such as N, S, B and P) can adjust the charge and spin density of carbon atoms, provide more active sites, and enrich the energy level structure of CQDs, thereby enhancing their fluorescent performance (Cortes, Falomir, Lancis Saez, & Mínguez-Vega, 2024; Gao et al., 2024; Vyas, Jaiswal, Choudhary, Kodgire, & Joshi, 2024). For instance, nitrogen-doped CQDs synthesized from pineapple peel and diethylenetriamine by hydrothermal method can serve as a fluorescent probe for the detection of tetracycline in pork (C. Wang, Sun, et al., 2022). Boron-doped CQDs prepared from citric acid and boric acid by hydropyrolysis can be used as a fluorescent probe to detect amoxicillin (X. Zhang, Ren, Ji, & Fan, 2020). Sulfur-doped CQDs were synthesized with citric acid and sodium sulfide, which can achieve the specific detection of trace Hg2+ in tap water (Wang et al., 2019). Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is a statistical classification technique that projects high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional space, maximizing the separation between different groups. The combination of multi-emission fluorescent probes with LDA allows for the high-specificity identification and rapid detection of multiple substances. For instance, a sensitized ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N, S co-doped CQDs and mercaptoacetic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots was developed, which can accurately recognize single and mixed tetracyclines, including tetracycline, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline and doxycycline in the combination with LDA model (Fan et al., 2023).

In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs (N, S-CQDs) emitting bright blue fluorescence were synthesized from citric acid and L-cysteine, which can be used as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the rapid detection of MXF, GAT and OFX in milk. After addition of MXF, GAT or OFX, the fluorescence at 440 nm was quenched due to the absorption of N, S-CQDs excitation light by MXF, GAT and OFX. And the -OH, -COOH and -NH2 groups on the surface of N, S-CQDs can form hydrogen bonds with quinolone antibiotics, leading to the fluorescence at 525 nm (MXF), 500 nm (GAT) and 515 nm (OFX) enhanced due to the aggregation induced emission. Good linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440) and the concentrations of quinolone antibiotics were obtained in the range of 0.1–100 mg L−1. MXF, GAT and OFX can be well recognized based on LDA model. Besides, test strips were prepared based on the ratiometric fluorescent probe, and the quinolone antibiotics in milk can be analyzed in combination with smartphone, proving this method has the potential to achieve real-time detection of MXF, GAT and OFX in milk.

Citric acid, L-cysteine, quinine sulfate, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, CdCl2, CrCl3, HgNO3, CoCl2, MgCl2, CuCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, NiCl2, PbCl2, AlCl3, BaCl2 and AgCl were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Standards of moxifloxacin (purity 99.3%), gatifloxacin (99.5%), ofloxacin (99.2%), tetracycline hydrochloride (99.2%), oxytetracycline (99.5%), ampicillin (99.4%), sulfamethoxazole (99.1%), sulfamethazine (98.9%), chlormequat (98.8%), boscalid (99.4%), dinotefuran (99.1%), novaluron (98.8%), cypermethrin (99.4%), cyhalothrin (99.5%), bifenthrin (99.4%), propargite (99.5%) and flonicamid (99.1%) were purchased from Tmstandard Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Chromatographic grade acetonitrile and analytical grade sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), respectively. Milk was randomly purchased from the local markets in Beijing, China. Ultrapure water was prepared by a Millipore purification system (Massachusetts, USA). The standard solutions of antibiotics, pesticides and metal ions were prepared in acetonitrile or ultra-pure water and stored at 4 °C before use.

N, S-CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid as carbon source and L-cysteine as nitrogen and sulfur sources. Citric acid (1.92 g) and L-cysteine (4.04 g) were dissolved in 50 mL ultra-pure water, and the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 11 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the filtrate was dialyzed for 2–3 days to remove the remaining reactants.

The absorbance of N, S-CQDs was recorded by UV–Vis spectrophotometer Evolution 300 (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of N, S-CQDs were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer RF-6000 (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology and particle size distribution of N, S-CQDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) JEM-F200 (JEOL, Japan). The crystalline structure of N, S-CQDs was characterized by Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer. The surface functional groups of N, S-CQDs were measured by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscope (FTIR) NicoletiS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The elemental composition of N, S-CQDs was explored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence lifetime was obtained by Stable Transient Fluorescence Spectrometer Fluoro Max-Plus (HORIBA, USA).

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N, S-CQDs was calculated by using 0.1M quinine sulfate as the standard:
Q Y = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
Where QY and QYR were the quantum yields of N, S-CQDs and quinine sulfate (54%), respectively; I and IR were the integrated fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; n and nR were the refractive indices (1.33 for both water and 0.1 M sulfuric acid) of water and 0.1 M sulfuric acid, respectively; A and AR were the absorbances of N, S-CQDs and quinine sulfate at the same excitation wavelength, which were below 0.05 in order to avoid the self-priming effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection based on N, S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After addition of 1.2 mL MXF, GAT and OFX solution into 4 mL probe solution, which was prepared by diluting N, S-CQD solution by 10 times, the fluorescence emission intensities at 440 nm (F440), 500 nm (F500), 515 nm (F515) and 525 nm (F525) were measured at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, and the fluorescence intensity ratios F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440 were calculated. The experiment was repeated for three times. LODs were calculated by LOD = 3σ/S (σ was the standard deviation of 10 blank samples, and S was the slope of the standard curve). LOQs were calculated by LOQ = 10LOD/3.

Antibiotics (tetracycline hydrochloride, oxytetracycline, ampicillin, sulfamethoxazole and sulfamethazine), pesticides (bifenthrin, cyhalothrin, chlormequat, boscalid, dinotefuran, flonicamid, novaluron, cypermethrin, propargite) and metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ba2+ and Ag+) solution at concentration of 50 mg L−1 were used to evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescence probe. F440, F500, F515 and F525 were measured at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, and the fluorescence intensity ratios F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440 were calculated. The experiment was conducted in triplicate.

Biomacromolecule in milk such as fat and protein may be adsorbed on the surface of quantum dots, which can interfere with the binding of antibiotics and quantum dots, affecting the accuracy of results. Therefore, pretreatment of milk sample is of great necessity. After addition of 5 mL acetonitrile and 2 g NaCl to 5 mL milk, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 3 min, and the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane. F440, F500, F515 and F525 were measured at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm after addition of MXF, GAT and OFX, and the fluorescence intensity ratios F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440 were calculated. The experiment was conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted detection based on fluorescent test strip
ce:section.ce:para: The test strip was prepared by dripped with 2 mL of the probe solution onto the filter paper and dried. After dripping 20 μL of milk extraction solution onto the test strip, photos were taken using the default camera app of a smartphone (iPhone 12) under a UV lamp with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The RGB values of the fluorescent color of the test strip were identified by Image J software. The ratios of green to blue values (G/B) were used to determine MXF and OFX concentrations.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, N, S-CQD displayed transparent and colorless solution under daylight light, which emit bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of N, S-CQD were 380 nm and 440 nm, respectively. Fluorescence quantum yield is an important index of luminescence efficiency, with higher quantum yield indicating higher efficiency of converting light energy into fluorescence. The relative quantum yield of N, S-CQDs was up to 31% (Table S1), indicating high luminescence efficiency. The characteristic functional groups of N, S-CQDs were measured by FTIR. It can be seen in Fig. 1B, stretching vibrations of O-H or N-H (3334 cm−1) and C=O (1649 cm−1) were observed. As shown in Fig. 1C that N, S-CQDs were uniformly spherical particles with an average diameter of 3.8 nm. N, S-CQDs were well dispersed in water, and no obvious agglomeration were observed. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N, S-CQDs (Fig. 1D) showed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 29.40°corresponding to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon, revealing its amorphous nature along with a low degree of crystallinity. Elements of N, S-CQDs were analyzed by XPS spectrum. The peaks at 163.7 eV, 284.9 eV, 400.0 eV and 531.4 eV were ascribed to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1E). There are three fitting peaks in the XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S1A), corresponding to C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.1 eV) and C=O (288.7 eV). The XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. S1B) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.6 eV, which contributed to C=O and C-O, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S1C) revealed two peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.4 eV, highlighting C-N and N-H, respectively (Fig. S1D). There are two fitting peaks in the XPS spectrum of S2p, corresponding to C-S-C S2P3/2 (163.5 eV) and C-S-C S2P1/2 (164.6 eV). The above results proved that CQDs were successfully doped with N and S elements, and the surface of N, S-CQDs contained hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl or amino groups, providing good water solubility.

The synthesis conditions for N, S-CQDs were optimized according to fluorescence intensity, including the molar ratio (citric acid: L-cysteine), synthesis temperature and reaction time. As shown in Fig. S2B, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs increased with the molar ratio increased from 0.1 to 0.3, however, decreased with the molar ratio increased from 0.3 to 0.5, which reached the highest value when the molar ratio was 0.3. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs gradually increased with the temperature increased from 120 °C to 200 °C (Fig. S2C). It can be seen from Fig. S2D that the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs increased with the synthesis time increased from 9 to 10 h, and then reached a plateau. Therefore, the synthesis condition parameters of N, S-CQDs were chosen as follow: molar ratio of citric acid to L-cysteine of 0.3, temperature at 200 °C, and synthesis time of 11 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of quinolone antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: N, S-CQDs were used as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of MXF, GAT and OFX. The fluorescence emission spectrum of N, S-CQDs was shown in Fig. S2A. The maximum emission wavelength of N, S-CQDs was 440 nm, which was shifted to 525 nm, 500 nm and 515 nm after addition of MXF, GAT and OFX, respectively. Fig. 2 showed the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs with varying concentrations of MXF, GAT and OFX. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was gradually decreased, while the fluorescence intensities at 525 nm (MXF), 500 nm (GAT), and 515 nm (OFX) gradually increased with the increase of quinolone antibiotic concentration. Good linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440) and the concentrations of MXF, GAT and OFX ranging from 0.1 to 100 mg L−1 were found, with the R2 greater than 0.9893 (Table S2). The LODs of MXF, GAT and OFX were 0.33, 0.57 and 0.77 μg L−1, and the LOQs were 1.11, 1.90 and 2.56 μg L−1, respectively. The results suggested that the N, S-CQD ratiometric fluorescent probe could achieve the quantitative analysis of MXF, GAT and OFX with low detection limits.

The recognition ability of N, S-CQD ratiometric fluorescent probes for different quinolone antibiotics was evaluated, including single and mixed quinolone antibiotics. The fluorescence intensities at 440 nm, 500 nm, 515 nm and 525 nm from different groups, with six replicates each group, were used to construct the LDA model. As shown in Fig. 3 , the separation of single and mixed quinolone antibiotics in aqueous solution can be realized using the LDA model, with classification accuracy rates greater than 0.989, which demonstrates the potential for detecting different quinolone antibiotics.

Currently, single-signal fluorescent probes have been successfully applied to the detection of quinolone antibiotics. For example, a fluorescence sensor based on metal–organic framework (MOF) [Co(apba)2(H2O)2] can achieve detection of MXF in milk, with fluorescence at 382 nm being quenched upon the addition of MXF (Tan et al., 2023). A fluorescence probe based on bovine serum capped ZnS nanoparticles (ZnS NPs) was developed for the detection of GAT, whose fluorescence at 296 nm can be quenched by GAT (L. Li et al., 2013). Green copper nanoclusters coated with sodium gluconate (Gl@Cu NCs) can be used as a fluorescence probe for the detection of OFX, with the fluorescence at 414 nm being significantly enhanced in the presence of OFX (Mohammad Hosseini & Sadeghi, 2024). However, single-signal fluorescent analysis suffers from low specificity and poor anti-interference ability, which may be easily affected by the background and probe concentration, resulting in false positive results. Dual-signal fluorescent probes exert superior anti-interference capabilities. Their fluorescence intensities in different emission bands change simultaneously, thereby the false positive results and detection error caused by background interference and other factors can be reduced according to the relative fluorescence intensity ratio. For instance, a fluorescence probe based on CQDs derived from polyvinyl alcohol was developed, whose fluorescence at 372 nm was quenched and the fluorescence at 508 nm was enhanced after addition of MXF, therefore, quantification of MXF in milk and eggs can be achieved according to fluorescence intensity ratio (F508/F372) (Sajwan, Kumar Himanshu, & Solanki, 2024). The real-time detection of OFX in daily drink was realized based on tri-doped graphene quantum dots (T-GQDs) ratiometric fluorescence probe (C. Wang, Qin, et al., 2023). The fluorescence at 414 nm was quenched, and the fluorescence at 498 nm was enhanced by the presence of OFX, and good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F498/F414) and OFX concentration was observed. In this study, N, S-CQDs dual-signal fluorescence probe was developed, which can selectively detect MXF, GAT, and OFX based on the fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440). Compared with other methods (Table 1 ), the ratiometric fluorescence probe method based on N, S-CQDs in this study has a wider linear range and a lower detection limit for the detection of MXF, GAT, and OFX.

The response time and pH were optimized by monitoring the fluorescence ratios (F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440). After addition of MXF, GAT and OFX, the fluorescence ratios increased rapidly within 30 s and remained stable within 30 min (Fig. 4 A). Therefore, the reaction time was set as 3 min in this study. The influence of pH on the fluorescence ratios of N, S-CQDs was assessed after adjusting the pH with HCl or NaOH. When the pH ranged from 4 to 14, the fluorescence ratios remained stable in the presence of MXF, GAT and OFX (Fig. 4B). Considering the pH of the solution was in the range of 4–5, no additional pH adjustment was conducted.

The fluorescence stability of N, S-CQDs was evaluated based on its fluorescence response toward quinolone antibiotics at room temperature (25 °C) and 4 °C. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the fluorescence intensity ratios of N, S-CQDs did not change significantly within 14 days at 4 °C and room temperature. These results demonstrated the highly stable N, S-CQD fluorescent probe has a sensitive and rapid response to MXF, GAT and OFX, which ensured the accuracy of the results.

The selectivity of the fluorescence probe was evaluated by adding 50 mg L−1 antibiotics (tetracycline hydrochloride, oxytetracycline, ampicillin, sulfamethoxazole and sulfamethazine), pesticides (bifenthrin, cyhalothrin, chlormequat, boscalid, dinotefuran, flonicamid, novaluron, cypermethrin, propargite) and metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ba2+ and Ag+) into the probe solution. As displayed in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence ratios F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440 were only significantly increased after addition of 5 mg L−1 MXF, GAT, and OFX, indicating the fluorescent probe had good selectivity for MXF, GAT, and OFX.

In order to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of N, S-CQDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs was 10.28 ns, which significantly decreased to 7.27, 9.84 and 9.01 ns after addition of MXF, GAT, and OFX, respectively. Dynamic quenching may lead to the decrease of fluorescence lifetime, however, the calculated dynamic quenching constant (5.4 × 1011 - 2.8 × 1012 mol−1 s−1) were obviously greater than the maximum dynamic quenching constant (2 × 1010 mol−1 s−1). The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CQDs, and ultraviolet absorbance spectrum of MXF, GAT, and OFX were demonstrated in Fig. 6B. It can be seen that the absorbance spectrum of MXF, GAT, and OFX obviously overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N, S-CQDs, indicating MXF, GAT and OFX can absorb the excitation light of N, S-CQDs. These results suggested fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between N, S-CQDs and MXF/GAT/OFX occurred, resulting in N, S-CQD fluorescence at 440 nm quenched. The functional groups -OH, -COOH and -NH2 on the surface of N, S-CQDs may form hydrogen bonds with MXF, GAT and OFX, and the fluorescence at 525 nm (MXF), 500 nm (GAT) and 515 nm (OFX) were enhanced due to the aggregation induced emission.

Recovery experiments of the N, S-CQD fluorescent probe were conducted at three spiked concentrations (0.1, 1 and 10 mg L−1). Satisfactory recoveries of MXF, GAT and OFX were obtained, ranging from 85 to 109%, with RSD less than 3.9% (Table S3). Good linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity ratios and the concentrations of MXF, GAT, and OFX in milk were found, with R2 greater than 0.9969 (Table S2). The LODs of MXF, GAT, and OFX in milk were 0.50, 0.12 and 0.19 μg L−1, respectively. The accuracy of N, S-CQD fluorescent probe was verified by traditional HPLC method, and the results obtained by HPLC were consistent with those obtained by N, S-CQD fluorescent probe, indicating the reliability of the N, S-CQD fluorescent probe in the detection of milk. As shown in Fig. 3B, a LDA model enabled the separation of single and mixed quinolone antibiotics from milk samples, achieving classification accuracy rates above 0.983. Therefore, the N, S-CQD ratiometric fluorescence probes have potential to analyze quinolone antibiotics in complex mixed actual samples.

A test strip based on N, S-CQD fluorescent probes was developed, which can achieve semiquantitative analysis of MXF and OFX in milk in the combination of smartphone. As shown in Fig. 7 , the color of the test strip under ultraviolet lamp gradually changed from bright blue to light green with the MXF concentration increased, which changed from bright blue to light blue with the OFX concentration increased. However, the color of the test strip under ultraviolet lamp has no obvious change before and after addition of GAT. And the relationships of the color and concentrations of MXF and OFX were further investigated. Satisfactory linear relationships between the G/B values and the concentration of MXF (in the range of 0.2–2 mg L−1), and the concentration of OFX (in the range of 0.1–3 mg L−1) were found (Table S4). Therefore, the concentrations of MXF and OFX in milk can be evaluated according to the fluorescent color under ultraviolet lamp combined with the smartphone. The method is simple and economical, which has the potential for in situ and real-time detection of MXF and OFX in milk.

In this study, N, S-CQDs emitting bright blue fluorescence were synthesized by citric acid and L-cysteine, which can be used as ratiometric fluorescent probes to achieve rapid detection of MXF, GAT, and OFX in milk. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer and aggregation induced emission, the blue fluorescence at 440 nm was significantly quenched and the green fluorescence at 525 nm, 500 nm and 515 nm were enhanced after addition of MXF, GAT, and OFX, respectively. Excellent fluorescence stability of N, S-CQDs was observed under different temperature and pH value. There was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratios (F525/F440, F500/F440 and F515/F440) and the concentrations of MXF, GAT, and OFX in the range of 0.1–100 mg L−1. The LODs ranged from 0.12 to 0.50 μg L−1, and different quinolones can be recognized based on the LDA model. A test strip based on ratiometric fluorescence probe was developed, which can achieve rapid detection of MXF and OFX in milk in combination with a smartphone. This method displayed high sensitivity, accuracy and good selectivity, which has the potential for convenient and real-time detection of MXF and OFX in milk samples.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42207461).
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Fang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Xiaoyun Li: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yuchen Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Hui Li: Investigation, Validation. Shanxue Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jiajun Han: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Wenwen Gong: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Dandan Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhiliang Yao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.
